persons.arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: with 7 Blandford officials in the staff lists and 2 Blandford biographies in the corpus, the family is locally common in Newfoundland, so a same-name relative (e.g. a son or brother also styled S. D.) could in principle hold some of these rows; and the chain is not perfectly clean — it contains 'S. Blandford | Sub-Collector' rows in 1920, 1921 and 1927 that overlap the Sheriff rows and almost certainly belong to a different person folded in upstream. Why it fails: the principal records track the biography across two distinct, unique, single-holder offices over 17 years — Minister of Agriculture and Mines from 1910 (bio: 'M.E.C. and min. of agric. and mines, 1909') through 1917, a Legislative Council listing in 1917 (bio: 'M.L.C. ... 1914'), then Sheriff of Newfoundland from 1918 (bio: 'sheriff of Newfoundland, 1917'). The full forename 'Sydney D.' matches exactly, the birth year 1868 makes him 42 at stint start, and there are 4 agreeing edition-years on the Sheriff office. No relative hypothesis survives a career that switches offices in lockstep with the printed biography.

evidence_cited.fact: S. D. Blandford | Minister of Agriculture and Mines
M.E.C. and min. of agric. and mines, 1909
S. D. Blandford | Sheriff of Newfoundland | Judicial Establishment

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous Sydney D. Blandford in Newfoundland (e.g. a separate biography with the same name and different career), or evidence that the Minister and Sheriff rows belong to two different men whose names were conflated by the chain builder.

unassigned_stints.reason: Single-initial 'D. Blandford', Sub-Collector of Customs 1911-1927. This career runs fully concurrent with Sydney D. Blandford's service as Minister of Agriculture and Mines (1910-1917) and Sheriff of Newfoundland (1918-1927); one man cannot hold an outport customs sub-collectorship and those offices simultaneously, and the biography never mentions any customs post (position_sim best 32, no agreeing edition-years). With 7 Blandford officials in the staff lists, this is almost certainly a different person, likely a relative. Initials overlap ('D') plus tenure overlap is exactly the weak evidence the constraints forbid merging on.

notes: The assigned stint chain is internally contaminated: 'S. Blandford | Sub-Collector' (1920, 1921) and 'S. Blandford | Collector' (1927) rows appear alongside the Sheriff rows in the same years and mirror the unassigned 'Blandford, D' customs career; they likely belong to a third Blandford (or to the same customs officer as the D. Blandford chain) and were bundled upstream. The identification of the biography with the Minister/Sheriff records is nonetheless certain. Reviewer may wish to split the customs rows out of the Sydney D chain.
